title: Phase diagram of the Kitaev-Heisenberg-$Γ$ model: Classical and quantum magnetism, frustration, and subdominant interactions
abstract: The Kitaev spin liquid provides a rare example of exactly solvable quantum spin liquid states. Intensive research over the past two decades has identified a variety of its candidate materials. In real materials, however, the Kitaev interaction is inevitably accompanied by additional magnetic interactions such as the Heisenberg and $Γ$ interactions. These interactions often induce magnetic ordering at low temperatures, making it essential to clarify their effects in the search for and design of Kitaev spin liquid candidate materials. In this study, we revisit the ground-state phase diagram of the Kitaev-Heisenberg-$Γ$ model from both classical and quantum perspectives, using state-of-the-art numerical techniques. In the classical case, we reveal a $zoo$ $of$ $noncollinear$ $orders$, where a variety of noncollinear multiple-$Q$ magnetic orders with and without incommensurate modulations emerge. In the quantum case, we unravel that quantum fluctuations suppress many of the competing orders found in the classical case, resulting in a reduced number of dominant incommensurate orders. We further identify $highly$ $frustrated$ regions, where spiral spin liquid states as well as new magnetically ordered states are potentially stabilized by other additional magnetic interactions. Our results provide a comprehensive perspective on the Kitaev-Heisenberg-$Γ$ model for both classical and quantum spins and offer a valuable guide not only for interpreting experimental results on candidate materials, but also for searching and designing new materials to realize the Kitaev spin liquid.
summary: This paper comprehensively maps the ground-state phase diagram of the Kitaev-Heisenberg-$\Gamma$ model, crucial for understanding real candidate materials. By comparing classical and quantum treatments, the authors reveal a complex interplay between magnetic interactions, showing that quantum effects significantly reduce the number of competing ordered states found classically. The results provide a vital guide for both interpreting experimental data and designing new spin liquid materials.
main_problem: The study aims to clarify the complex ground-state phase diagram of the Kitaev-Heisenberg-$\Gamma$ model, specifically addressing how additional magnetic interactions perturb the ideal Kitaev spin liquid state.
main_result: Classically, the system exhibits a 'zoo' of noncollinear multiple-$Q$ magnetic orders, while quantum fluctuations suppress many of these competing classical phases, suggesting stabilized spiral spin liquid states in highly frustrated regions.
method: The research employs advanced numerical techniques, including gradient descent optimization for classical spins and the Pseudo-fermion Functional Renormalization Group (PFFRG) method for quantum spins.
model_or_system: The model is the Kitaev-Heisenberg-$\Gamma$ Hamiltonian applied to a 2D honeycomb lattice. It incorporates three types of interactions ($J, K, \Gamma$) and is analyzed across both classical spin configurations and $S=1/2$ quantum spins.
key_observables: Spin structure factor S(q), total scalar spin chirality $\chi_{	ext{tot}}$, static spin susceptibility $\chi_{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q})$, and the energy per site $\epsilon$.
important_parameters: The coupling constants $(J, K, \Gamma)$ are varied via parameters $(	heta, \phi)$, allowing mapping of the phase diagram across different interaction regimes.
main_assumptions: For quantum calculations, the zero-temperature limit is used; for classical analysis, optimization relies on minimizing energy using automatic differentiation techniques.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate ground-state phase diagrams showing various magnetic phases (e.g., Néel, FM, QSL) as a function of coupling parameters, alongside plots detailing spin susceptibilities and eigenvalue distributions across the parameter space.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the model by first establishing the Hamiltonian and then performing separate, detailed analyses for classical ground states using variational minimization, followed by quantum calculations utilizing PFFRG to map out the full phase diagram.
why_interesting: The explicit comparison between classical and quantum predictions regarding magnetic ordering is highly relevant as it elucidates how quantum fluctuations stabilize or suppress competing ordered phases in strongly correlated magnets.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ground-state phase diagrams of the Kitaev-Heisenberg-Γ model in Eq. (1), parametrized by polar angle θ and azimuthal angle ϕ as defined in Eq. (2), for (a) classical spins for Γ ≥0, (b) quantum spins for Γ ≥0, and (c) quantum spins for Γ ≤0. In (a), (J, K, Γ) can be transformed to (−J, −K, −Γ) by a site-dependent time-reversal, and thus only the region with Γ ≥0 is shown. Calculations are carried out at 314620 parameter points. The region enclosed by the red dashed line indicates where the Luttinger-Tisza method loses its validity. The hatched regions indicate where incommensurate magnetic order is suggested. In (b) and (c), the black ticks on the outer circles indicate the phase...
FIG. 2. Enlarged view of the lower quadrant of Fig. 1(a). The nota- tions are common to those in Fig. 1(a).

Figure 1(a) shows the phase diagram in the classical case obtained from our numerical minimization. As noted in Sec. II A, for classical spins, it is sufficient to determine the phase diagram only for Γ ≥0. We obtain dominant phases, FM, N´eel AFM, zigzag, stripy, and vortex (also called 120◦), in agreement with previous studies [94, 95]. However, in the regions where the Luttinger-Tisza method is no longer valid [enclosed by the red dashed line in Fig. 1(a)], we identify highly competitive and complicated phase structures that are remarkably different from those reported in the previous stud- ies. To discuss this region in detail, Fig. 2 shows an enlarged view of the lower quadrant (5/4...

FIG. 3. (a) Second-order derivative, (b) absolute value of the total scalar spin chirality χtot, and (c) system size dependence of the en- ergy per site ε, calculated from the optimized spin configurations. As in Fig. 2, only the lower quadrant of the phase diagram is shown.

FIG. 4. Typical data of phases (a) 6, (b) 6′, (c) 8, (d) 8′, and (e) 24. The left panels show the optimized spin configurations. The colors of the arrows encode the magnitude of the out-of-plane component: [111] and [¯1¯1¯1] directions are shown in red and blue, respectively. Gray represents the in-plane component. The color code and spin axes are shown in (a). Orange dotted lines indicate the magnetic unit cells. The right panels show q dependences of S(q)/max[S(q)] in logarithmic scale. The color code is shown in (a). The inner white dotted and outer white solid lines represent the first Brillouin zone and the enlarged zone including up to the third one, respectively.
